Title: The Bleepforge manifest contract
Summary: A JSON file at your Godot project root that describes every BleepforgeResource subclass — its kind, fields, key, and editor surface. Bleepforge reads it to drive generic authoring surfaces in v0.2.7+.
Body:
## What it is

`bleepforge_manifest.json` is a JSON file that lives at your Godot project's root (alongside `project.godot`). It describes the schema of every Bleepforge-authored resource type your project defines:

- Which kind of entry it is (one of four: `domain`, `discriminatedFamily`, `foldered`, `enumKeyed`).
- What fields it has (12 supported types: `string`, `multiline`, `int`, `float`, `bool`, `enum`, `flag`, `ref`, `array`, `subresource`, `texture`, `scene`).
- How the editor should render it (`view: "list" | "cards" | "graph"`, optional `overrideUi` component name).

Bleepforge reads the manifest from your active project's Godot root and uses it to drive generic editor surfaces in v0.2.7+. In v0.2.6 the editor only **reads + validates** the manifest — it surfaces detection state in [Diagnostics → Manifest](/diagnostics/manifest) but doesn't yet drive any UI. The hardcoded surfaces (Items, Quests, NPCs, etc.) work as before.

## Who writes it

The **godot-lib** companion plugin. You drop it into your Godot project's `addons/bleepforge/` folder, enable it in Project Settings → Plugins, and on every editor load the plugin reflects over your `BleepforgeResource` subclasses + their registries to emit the manifest. There's also a `Tools → Re-export Bleepforge manifest` menu item for manual re-emit.

If you don't have godot-lib installed, no manifest gets emitted — and Bleepforge's existing surfaces (the seven game-domain editors) work fine without it. Manifest is **additive**, not required.

## Where it sits in your project

```text
<your-godot-project>/
├── project.godot
├── bleepforge_manifest.json     ← emitted by godot-lib
├── addons/
│   └── bleepforge/              ← copy from godot-lib/addons/bleepforge/
│       ├── plugin.cfg
│       ├── BleepforgePlugin.cs
│       ├── runtime/             ← BleepforgeResource + 4 registry base classes
│       └── editor/              ← ManifestEmitter + ManifestModel
└── ... your scenes, .tres files, etc
```

## What's in it

Top-level shape:

```json
{
  "schemaVersion": 1,
  "domains": [ ... ],
  "subResources": [ ... ]
}
```

Each `domains[]` entry is one of four kinds, each with the shape that fits its access pattern:

- **`domain`** — single instance per file, flat folder. Identity is a field on the resource (e.g. `Slug`, `Id`, `NpcId`). Most domains are this kind.
- **`discriminatedFamily`** — base class with N variants, picked by an enum field on the base. Variants add fields on top of base. Useful for type-discriminated unions (Items with QuestItem subtype, etc.).
- **`foldered`** — per-folder grouping discovered by walking the project tree. Composite ids `<folder>/<basename>`. Useful for content where the same type lives in multiple sibling folders (one per speaker, one per character model).
- **`enumKeyed`** — exactly one instance per enum value. Useful for fixed-cardinality registries (Factions, Locations, etc.) where the set of valid keys is known at compile time.

Each `subResources[]` entry is a sub-resource type referenced from a domain's `array` or `subresource` field.

## What you do with it

In v0.2.6 — **nothing yet**. The Diagnostics → Manifest tab surfaces what Bleepforge sees so you can verify your library setup is correct, but it doesn't drive any editor surface yet.

In v0.2.7+ — the manifest will drive generic `<DomainList>` and `<DomainEdit>` surfaces for any domain you declare. Bleepforge will edit any project that has a manifest, with no per-domain editor code required. Bespoke UIs (the FoB DialogGraph, BalloonCard, etc.) keep working via the `overrideUi` field — generic is the default for new domains, override is the escape hatch.

## The four states the editor surfaces

Diagnostics → Manifest shows one of four states:

- **OK** (green) — manifest exists, parses cleanly, validates against the schema. Per-domain table follows.
- **Not detected** (amber) — sync-mode project but no `bleepforge_manifest.json` at the root. Normal for projects without godot-lib installed.
- **Error** (red) — file exists but failed to parse or validate. Almost always means a library version skew (your godot-lib is older than your Bleepforge editor or vice versa). Per-issue list follows.
- **Not applicable** (muted) — notebook-mode project (no Godot root by design) or no Godot root configured.

## See also

- [godot-lib — the companion library](/help/under-the-hood/godot-lib) — install flow, why first-open needs the manual C# build, what the four registry base classes do.
- [.tres is truth, JSON is cache](/help/under-the-hood/tres-is-truth) — the data model the manifest sits on top of.
